concepts.name: pre-conditioned lasso
concepts.description: A variant of the lasso where the outcome variable is first denoised using supervised principal components, then the lasso is applied with the denoised outcome as the target. All features are used in the lasso fit, not just those retained in thresholding.
concepts.quote: In this approach, one first computes the supervised principal component predictor ŷi for each observation in the training set (with the threshold selected by cross-validation). Then we apply the lasso with ŷi as the outcome variable, in place of the usual outcome yi.

concepts.name: thresholded partial least squares
concepts.description: A variant of partial least squares where features are thresholded based on their correlation with the outcome before applying PLS, similar to supervised principal components.
concepts.quote: Supervised principal components and thresholded PLS work best over a wide range of the number of selected features, with the former showing consistently lower test errors.

concepts.name: two-sample t-statistic for genes
concepts.description: A standardized measure of differential expression between two groups for each gene, computed as the difference in mean expression divided by pooled standard error.
concepts.quote: To identify informative genes, we construct a two-sample t-statistic for each gene.

concepts.name: pooled within-group standard error
concepts.description: The standard error estimate for a gene computed by pooling variance estimates across both treatment groups.
concepts.quote: The quantity sej is the pooled within-group standard error for gene j:

concepts.name: permutation distribution for hypothesis testing
concepts.description: A nonparametric approach to computing p-values by permuting sample labels and computing test statistics for each permutation, avoiding distributional assumptions.
concepts.quote: An attractive alternative approach is to use the permutation distribution, since it avoids assumptions about the distribution of the data. We compute (in principle) all K = (58 choose 14) permutations of the sample labels, and for each permutation k compute the t-statistics tk_j.

concepts.name: pooled permutation p-values
concepts.description: P-values computed by pooling test statistic results across all genes rather than computing separate permutation distributions for each gene, yielding more granular p-values.
concepts.quote: To exploit the fact that the genes are similar (e.g., measured on the same scale), we can instead pool the results for all genes in computing the p-values.

concepts.name: family-wise error rate
concepts.description: The probability of making at least one false rejection among all hypothesis tests, a conservative measure of overall error in multiple testing.
concepts.quote: The family-wise error rate (FWER) is the probability of at least one false rejection, and is a commonly used overall measure of error.

concepts.name: bonferroni method
concepts.description: A multiple testing correction that controls family-wise error rate by testing each hypothesis at significance level α/M, where M is the number of tests.
concepts.quote: One of the simplest approaches to multiple testing is the Bonferroni method. It makes each individual test more stringent, in order to make the FWER equal to at most α: we reject H0j if pj < α/M.

concepts.name: false discovery rate
concepts.description: The expected proportion of false positives among all features called significant, defined as E[V/max(R,1)] where V is false positives and R is total rejections.
concepts.quote: In the microarray setting, this is the expected proportion of genes that are incorrectly called significant, among the R genes that are called significant.

concepts.name: benjamini-hochberg procedure
concepts.description: A procedure for controlling false discovery rate at level α by ordering p-values and finding the largest index where p(j) ≤ jα/M, then rejecting all hypotheses with smaller p-values.
concepts.quote: Benjamini and Hochberg (1995) first proposed the notion of false discovery rate, and gave a testing procedure (Algorithm 18.2) whose FDR is bounded by a user-defined level α.

concepts.name: plug-in estimate of false discovery rate
concepts.description: A direct estimate of FDR computed as the average number of permutation values exceeding a threshold divided by the number of observed values exceeding that threshold.
concepts.quote: The total number of permutation values |tk_j| equal or greater than 4.101 is 1518, for an average of 1518/1000 = 1.518 per permutation. Thus a direct estimate of the false discovery rate is FDR̂ = 1.518/11 ≈ 14%.

concepts.name: significance analysis of microarrays
concepts.description: A method for computing asymmetric significance cutpoints by plotting ordered test statistics against expected order statistics from permutations and finding where points leave a band of width Δ from the 45° line.
concepts.quote: The significance analysis of microarrays (SAM) approach offers a way of doing this. The basis of the SAM method is shown in Figure 18.20. On the vertical axis we have plotted the ordered test statistics t(1) ≤ t(2) ≤ ... ≤ t(M), while the horizontal axis shows the expected order statistics from the permutations of the data.

concepts.name: asymmetric cutpoints in multiple testing
concepts.description: Using different significance thresholds for positive and negative test statistics when most differentially expressed features change in one direction.
concepts.quote: In the testing methods described above, we used the absolute value of the test statistic tj, and hence applied the same cut-points to both positive and negative values of the statistic. In some experiments, it might happen that most or all of the differentially expressed genes change in the positive direction (or all in the negative direction). For this situation it is advantageous to derive separate cut-points for the two cases.

concepts.name: positive false discovery rate
concepts.description: A variant of FDR defined as E[V/R | R > 0], the expected false discovery rate conditional on making at least one rejection.
concepts.quote: First we need to define the positive false discovery rate (pFDR) as

concepts.name: mixture model for hypothesis testing
concepts.description: A Bayesian framework where each test statistic comes from either the null distribution F0 with probability π0 or the alternative distribution F1 with probability 1-π0.
concepts.quote: We define a random variable Zj which equals 0 if the jth null hypothesis is true, and 1 otherwise. We assume that each pair (tj, Zj) are i.i.d random variables with

concepts.name: q-value
concepts.description: The smallest false discovery rate over all rejection regions that would reject a given test statistic, providing a local measure of significance for each feature.
concepts.quote: The q-value (Storey, 2003) of a test statistic tj is defined to be the smallest FDR over all rejection regions that reject tj. That is, for symmetric rejection regions, the q-value for tj = 2 is defined to be the FDR for the rejection region Γ = {-(∞, -2) ∪ (2, ∞)}.

concepts.name: local false discovery rate
concepts.description: The positive FDR for an infinitesimal rejection region surrounding a specific test statistic value, providing the most local measure of significance.
concepts.quote: The local false discovery rate (Efron and Tibshirani, 2002) at t = t0 is defined to be

concepts.name: supervised principal components for feature selection
concepts.description: A method that thresholds features based on univariate association with outcome, then performs principal components analysis on retained features and uses components in supervised learning.
concepts.quote: Supervised principal components can yield lower test errors than competing methods, as shown in Figure 18.16. However, it does not always produce a sparse model involving only a small number of features (genes).

concepts.name: denoising the outcome variable
concepts.description: Using a first-stage predictor to reduce noise in the outcome before applying a second-stage method, reducing overfitting with many noisy features.
concepts.quote: The idea is that by first denoising the outcome variable, the lasso should not be as adversely affected by the large number of noise features.

concepts.name: feature assessment without prediction models
concepts.description: Assessing significance of individual features for scientific understanding rather than building multivariate predictive models, focusing on hypothesis testing.
concepts.quote: Thus our goal is to assess the significance of individual features. This assessment is usually done without the use of a multivariate predictive model like those in the first part of this chapter.

concepts.name: type-I error rate
concepts.description: The probability of falsely rejecting a true null hypothesis, typically set at α = 0.05 for individual tests.
concepts.quote: This test has type-I error equal to α; that is, the probability of falsely rejecting H0j is α.

concepts.name: positive dependence between tests
concepts.description: A correlation structure where Pr(Aj|Ak) > Pr(Aj), meaning tests are more likely to reject together, which reduces family-wise error rate below the independent case.
concepts.quote: On the other hand, if the tests have positive dependence, that is Pr(Aj|Ak) > Pr(Aj), then the FWER will be less than (1 - (1 - α)^M). Positive dependence between tests often occurs in practice, in particular in genomic studies.

concepts.name: bh rejection threshold
concepts.description: The p-value cutoff p(L) determined by the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure, where L is the largest index satisfying p(j) ≤ jα/M.
concepts.quote: Reject all hypotheses H0j for which pj ≤ p(L), the BH rejection threshold.

concepts.name: expected order statistics from permutations
concepts.description: The average of ordered test statistics across permutations, denoted ť(j) = (1/K)Σk tk(j), used as null reference in SAM procedure.
concepts.quote: On the vertical axis we have plotted the ordered test statistics t(1) ≤ t(2) ≤ ... ≤ t(M), while the horizontal axis shows the expected order statistics from the permutations of the data: ť(j) = (1/K) ΣK k=1 tk(j)

concepts.name: sam tuning parameter delta
concepts.description: The width parameter Δ in SAM that defines parallel bands around the 45° line, controlling the distance at which genes are called significant.
concepts.quote: Two lines are drawn, parallel to the 45° line, Δ units away. Starting at the origin and moving to the right, we find the first place that the genes leave the band. This defines the upper cutpoint Chi and all genes beyond that point are called significant.

concepts.name: bayesian interpretation of fdr
concepts.description: Under a mixture model, the pFDR equals the posterior probability that the null hypothesis is true given the test statistic falls in the rejection region.
concepts.quote: Hence under the mixture model (18.51), the pFDR is the posterior probability that the null hypothesis it true, given that test statistic falls in the rejection region for the test; that is, given that we reject the null hypothesis

concepts.name: radiation sensitivity gene expression study
concepts.description: A microarray study with 58 patient samples (44 normal, 14 radiation-sensitive) and 12,625 genes, used to identify genes associated with adverse radiation response.
concepts.quote: Consider, for example, the microarray data in Table 18.4, taken from a study on the sensitivity of cancer patients to ionizing radiation treatment (Rieger et al., 2004). Each row consists of the expression of genes in 58 patient samples: 44 samples were from patients with a normal reaction, and 14 from patients who had a severe reaction to radiation.

concepts.name: diagonal linear discriminant analysis for high dimensions
concepts.description: A variant of LDA that uses a diagonal covariance matrix, appropriate when p >> N and features can be assumed independent.
concepts.quote: Levina (2002) does some mathematical analysis comparing diagonal LDA to full LDA, as p, N → ∞ with p > N. She shows that with reasonable assumptions diagonal LDA has a lower asymptotic error rate than full LDA.

concepts.name: nearest shrunken centroids classifier
concepts.description: A classification method that shrinks class centroids toward the overall mean, effectively performing feature selection by setting some centroid components to zero.
concepts.quote: Tibshirani et al. (2001a) and Tibshirani et al. (2003) proposed the nearest shrunken-centroid classifier.

concepts.name: fused lasso
concepts.description: A variant of lasso that penalizes both absolute values of coefficients and differences between adjacent coefficients, useful for ordered features like genomic data.
concepts.quote: The fused lasso was proposed by Tibshirani et al. (2005)

concepts.name: elastic net
concepts.description: A regularization method combining L1 (lasso) and L2 (ridge) penalties, providing both feature selection and grouping of correlated features.
concepts.quote: while Zou and Hastie (2005) introduced the elastic net.

The idea is that by first denoising the outcome variable, the lasso should not be as adversely affected by the large number of noise features.
Supervised principal components and thresholded PLS work best over a wide range of the number of selected features, with the former showing consistently lower test errors.
Although a linear model in the first 50 features is optimal, the lasso (green) is adversely affected by the large number of noisy features, and starts overfitting when far fewer are in the model.

Figure 18.17 shows that pre-conditioning (purple curve) has been successful here, yielding much lower test error than the usual lasso, and as low (in this case) as for supervised principal components. It also can achieve this using less features.

Suppose that M identical simple hypothesis tests are performed with the i.i.d. statistics t1, ..., tM and rejection region Γ. We define a random variable Zj which equals 0 if the jth null hypothesis is true, and 1 otherwise.
The q-value (Storey, 2003) of a test statistic tj is defined to be the smallest FDR over all rejection regions that reject tj.
This is the (positive) FDR for an infinitesimal rejection region surrounding the value tj = t0.
The q-value for tj = 5 will be smaller than that for tj = 2, reflecting the fact that tj = 5 is more significant than tj = 2.

relationships.source: supervised principal components for feature selection
relationships.quote: How do the test errors of the two methods compare on the simulated example of the last section? Figure 18.17 shows the test errors for one realization from model (18.37) for the lasso, supervised principal components, and the pre-conditioned lasso

relationships.target: supervised principal components for feature selection
relationships.quote: Pre-conditioning can be applied in a variety of settings, using initial estimates other than supervised principal components and post-processors other than the lasso.
Thus our goal is to assess the significance of individual features. This assessment is usually done without the use of a multivariate predictive model like those in the first part of this chapter.
One of the simplest approaches to multiple testing is the Bonferroni method. It makes each individual test more stringent, in order to make the FWER equal to at most α: we reject H0j if pj < α/M.
Let Aj be the event that H0j is falsely rejected; by definition Pr(Aj) = α. The family-wise error rate (FWER) is the probability of at least one false rejection
We must remember that the FDR is not the same as type-I error, for which 0.05 is the customary choice.
if the tests have positive dependence, that is Pr(Aj|Ak) > Pr(Aj), then the FWER will be less than (1 - (1 - α)^M)
